docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Strands Agents jsii SDK

Use Strands Agents from Python, Java, Go, and C#

Strands Agents jsii SDK provides multi-language bindings for the Strands Agents framework using jsii. Write your AI agents in any language — Python, Java, Go, or C# — with the same API.

docs.title: ✨ Features
docs.text: Feature Status 10 Model Providers Bedrock, Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama, Mistral, LiteLLM, Writer, LlamaAPI, SageMaker Tool Execution Define tools in your language, model calls them Agent-as-Tool Pass agent context to tools via ToolContext Multi-Agent Wrap agents as tools for delegation patterns Conversation Management Sliding window, null manager Callback Handlers Hook into model/tool/agent lifecycle events Multi-Language Python, Java, Go, C# from single TypeScript source

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Example
🏗️ How It Works
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Your Code<br/>Python / Java / Go / C#] -->|jsii| B[TypeScript SDK<br/>Agent Loop]
    B -->|sync subprocess| C[Model Provider<br/>Bedrock / OpenAI / ...]
    C -->|response| B
    B -->|tool_use| D[Your Tool Code<br/>Runs in your language]
    D -->|result| B
    B -->|final response| A

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Complete API reference for the Strands Agents jsii SDK.

docs.title: StrandsAgent
docs.text:
The main agent class that orchestrates model calls and tool execution.

class StrandsAgent:
    def __init__(self, config: AgentConfig)
    def invoke(self, prompt: str) -> AgentResponse
Method Returns Description invoke(prompt) AgentResponse Run the agent loop with a prompt

docs.title: AgentConfig
docs.text:
Configuration for creating an agent.

class AgentConfig:
    def __init__(
        self,
        model: ModelProvider,
        system_prompt: str = "",
        tools: List[ToolDefinition] = [],
        max_cycles: int = 50,
        conversation_manager: ConversationManager = None,
        callback_handler: CallbackHandler = None,
    )
Parameter Type Default Description model ModelProvider required LLM provider system_prompt str "" System instructions tools List[ToolDefinition] [] Available tools max_cycles int 50 Max agent loop iterations conversation_manager ConversationManager None Message management strategy callback_handler CallbackHandler None Lifecycle event handler

docs.title: AgentResponse
docs.text:
Response from an agent invocation.

class AgentResponse:
    text: str           # Final text response
    input_tokens: int   # Input tokens used
    output_tokens: int  # Output tokens generated

docs.title: ModelProvider (Abstract)
docs.text:
class ModelProvider:
    provider_name: str   # Provider identifier
    model_id: str        # Model identifier

docs.title: ToolSpecification
docs.text:
class ToolSpecification:
    def __init__(self, name: str, description: str, input_schema: str)
Parameter Type Description name str Tool name (used by model) description str What the tool does input_schema str JSON Schema string for parameters

docs.title: ToolDefinition (Abstract)
docs.text:
class ToolDefinition:
    def __init__(self, spec: ToolSpecification)
    def execute(self, input_json: str) -> str  # Override this

docs.title: ContextAwareToolDefinition (Abstract)
docs.text:
class ContextAwareToolDefinition:
    def __init__(self, spec: ToolSpecification)
    def execute_with_context(self, input_json: str, context: ToolContext) -> str

docs.title: ToolContext
docs.text:
class ToolContext:
    agent: StrandsAgent  # The calling agent

docs.title: AgentTool
docs.text:
class AgentTool(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self, name: str, description: str, agent: StrandsAgent)

docs.title: SlidingWindowConversationManager
docs.text:
class SlidingWindowConversationManager:
    def __init__(self, window_size: int = 40)

docs.title: NullConversationManager
docs.text:
class NullConversationManager:
    def __init__(self)

docs.title: CallbackHandler (Abstract)
docs.text:
class CallbackHandler:
    def on_model_start(self, messages_json: str) -> None
    def on_model_end(self, response_json: str) -> None
    def on_model_error(self, error: str) -> None
    def on_tool_start(self, tool_name: str, input_json: str) -> None
    def on_tool_end(self, tool_name: str, result: str) -> None
    def on_tool_error(self, tool_name: str, error: str) -> None
    def on_agent_start(self, prompt: str) -> None
    def on_agent_end(self, response_json: str) -> None

docs.title: How jsii Enables Multi-Language AI Agents
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph "Your Application"
        PY[Python App]
        JV[Java App]
        GO[Go App]
        CS[C# App]
    end

    subgraph "jsii Runtime"
        RT[jsii Kernel<br/>Node.js subprocess]
        TS[TypeScript SDK<br/>agent.ts + model.ts]
    end

    subgraph "Model Providers"
        BR[Amazon Bedrock]
        AN[Anthropic API]
        OA[OpenAI API]
        OL[Ollama Local]
        GM[Gemini API]
        MI[Mistral API]
    end

    PY & JV & GO & CS -->|jsii protocol| RT
    RT --> TS
    TS -->|HTTP/AWS SDK| BR & AN & OA & OL & GM & MI

docs.title: The Agent Loop
docs.text:
The core agent loop runs inside the TypeScript SDK, managed by jsii:

sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Your Code (Python/Java/Go/C#)
    participant jsii as jsii Runtime
    participant Agent as Agent Loop (TypeScript)
    participant Model as Model Provider
    participant Tool as Your Tool (Python/Java/Go/C#)

    App->>jsii: agent.invoke("query")
    jsii->>Agent: process(messages)

    loop Until no more tool_use
        Agent->>Model: converse(messages)
        Model-->>Agent: response (text + tool_use)

        opt If tool_use
            Agent->>jsii: tool.execute(input)
            jsii->>Tool: execute(input_json)
            Tool-->>jsii: result_json
            jsii-->>Agent: tool_result
        end
    end

    Agent-->>jsii: final response
    jsii-->>App: AgentResponse

docs.title: 1. Synchronous Model Calls via Subprocess
docs.text:
Model providers use child_process.execSync to call AWS CLI or curl:

Agent Loop → execSync("aws bedrock-runtime converse ...") → JSON response

This is intentionally synchronous because jsii doesn't support async/await across language boundaries. The trade-off: simpler API, no callback hell.

docs.title: 2. Tools Execute in Your Language
docs.text:
When the model requests a tool call, the jsii runtime calls back into your language:

TypeScript Agent → jsii callback → Python ToolDefinition.execute() → jsii → TypeScript

Your tool code runs natively in Python/Java/Go/C#. No serialization overhead beyond JSON.

docs.title: 3. Single TypeScript Source → 4 Language Bindings
docs.text:
src/agent.ts + src/model.ts
    ↓ jsii compile
.jsii assembly (type metadata)
    ↓ jsii-pacmak
dist/python/  dist/java/  dist/go/  dist/dotnet/

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
strands-jsii-test/
├── src/
│   ├── agent.ts        # StrandsAgent, AgentConfig, tools, callbacks
│   ├── model.ts        # All 10 model providers
│   └── index.ts        # Public API exports
├── .jsii               # Compiled type assembly
├── dist/               # Generated language bindings
│   ├── python/
│   ├── java/
│   ├── go/
│   └── dotnet/
├── package.json        # jsii configuration
└── tsconfig.json

docs.title: Comparison with Python SDK
docs.text: Aspect Python SDK jsii SDK Languages Python only Python, Java, Go, C# Model calls Async (boto3, httpx) Sync (subprocess) Streaming ✅ Full streaming ❌ Full response only Tool execution Python native Native in each language Agent loop Python TypeScript (via jsii) Dependencies pip packages per provider Node.js + jsii only Performance Optimal ~50ms overhead per model call

docs.title: Language-Specific Setup
Building from Source
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/cagataycali/strands-jsii-test.git
cd strands-jsii-test

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Compile TypeScript → jsii assembly
npx jsii

# Generate language bindings
npx jsii-pacmak

# Output in dist/
ls dist/
# python/  java/  go/  dotnet/

docs.title: Node.js Requirement
docs.text:
Node.js is required at runtime

jsii uses a Node.js subprocess to execute the TypeScript agent loop. Node.js ≥ 20 must be installed on the machine where your code runs.

The jsii runtime automatically manages the Node.js process — you don't need to write any JavaScript.

docs.title: Amazon Bedrock
docs.text:
# Configure AWS credentials
aws configure
# Or set environment variables
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
export AWS_REGION=us-west-2

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get a Strands Agent running in 5 minutes from any language.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Node.js ≥ 20 (jsii runtime requirement)
  • Python ≥ 3.9, Java ≥ 11, Go ≥ 1.21, or .NET ≥ 8.0
  • AWS credentials (for Bedrock) or API keys for other providers

docs.title: 1. Install
2. Create an Agent
3. Add a Tool
4. Use Different Providers
docs.text:
# OpenAI
from strands_agents_jsii import OpenAIModelProvider, OpenAIModelConfig
model = OpenAIModelProvider(OpenAIModelConfig("gpt-4o"))

# Anthropic Direct
from strands_agents_jsii import AnthropicModelProvider, AnthropicModelConfig
model = AnthropicModelProvider(AnthropicModelConfig("claude-sonnet-4-20250514"))

# Ollama (Local)
from strands_agents_jsii import OllamaModelProvider, OllamaModelConfig
model = OllamaModelProvider(OllamaModelConfig("qwen3:8b"))

# Google Gemini
from strands_agents_jsii import GeminiModelProvider, GeminiModelConfig
model = GeminiModelProvider(GeminiModelConfig("gemini-2.5-flash"))

docs.title: Callback Handlers
docs.text: Hook into agent lifecycle events for logging, metrics, cost tracking, and observability.

docs.title: CallbackHandler Interface
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import CallbackHandler

class MyHandler(CallbackHandler):
    def on_model_start(self, messages_json):
        """Called before model invocation"""
        pass

    def on_model_end(self, response_json):
        """Called after model response"""
        pass

    def on_model_error(self, error):
        """Called on model error"""
        pass

    def on_tool_start(self, tool_name, input_json):
        """Called before tool execution"""
        pass

    def on_tool_end(self, tool_name, result):
        """Called after tool execution"""
        pass

    def on_tool_error(self, tool_name, error):
        """Called on tool error"""
        pass

    def on_agent_start(self, prompt):
        """Called when agent.invoke() is called"""
        pass

    def on_agent_end(self, response_json):
        """Called when agent.invoke() completes"""
        pass

docs.title: Logging Handler
docs.text:
import json
from datetime import datetime

class LoggingHandler(CallbackHandler):
    def on_agent_start(self, prompt):
        print(f"[{datetime.now()}] 🚀 Agent started: {prompt[:50]}...")

    def on_tool_start(self, name, input_json):
        print(f"[{datetime.now()}] 🔧 Tool: {name}")

    def on_tool_end(self, name, result):
        print(f"[{datetime.now()}] ✅ {name} completed")

    def on_tool_error(self, name, error):
        print(f"[{datetime.now()}] ❌ {name} failed: {error}")

    def on_agent_end(self, response_json):
        resp = json.loads(response_json)
        tokens = resp.get("inputTokens", 0) + resp.get("outputTokens", 0)
        print(f"[{datetime.now()}] 🏁 Done ({tokens} tokens)")

docs.title: Cost Tracker
docs.text:
class CostTracker(CallbackHandler):
    # Approximate pricing per 1M tokens
    PRICING = {
        "claude-sonnet-4": {"input": 3.0, "output": 15.0},
        "gpt-4o": {"input": 2.5, "output": 10.0},
    }

    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.total_input = 0
        self.total_output = 0
        self.total_cost = 0.0

    def on_model_end(self, response_json):
        resp = json.loads(response_json)
        input_tokens = resp.get("inputTokens", 0)
        output_tokens = resp.get("outputTokens", 0)
        self.total_input += input_tokens
        self.total_output += output_tokens

        # Estimate cost (using sonnet pricing as default)
        pricing = self.PRICING.get("claude-sonnet-4", {"input": 3, "output": 15})
        cost = (input_tokens * pricing["input"] + output_tokens * pricing["output"]) / 1_000_000
        self.total_cost += cost

    def report(self):
        return {
            "total_input_tokens": self.total_input,
            "total_output_tokens": self.total_output,
            "estimated_cost_usd": round(self.total_cost, 4),
        }

docs.title: Tool Auditor
docs.text:
class ToolAuditor(CallbackHandler):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.tool_calls = []

    def on_tool_start(self, name, input_json):
        self.tool_calls.append({
            "tool": name,
            "input": input_json,
            "start_time": datetime.now().isoformat(),
        })

    def on_tool_end(self, name, result):
        # Update last matching call
        for call in reversed(self.tool_calls):
            if call["tool"] == name and "result" not in call:
                call["result"] = result[:500]
                call["end_time"] = datetime.now().isoformat()
                break

docs.title: Using Handlers
docs.text:
tracker = CostTracker()

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="Be helpful.",
    callback_handler=tracker,
))

agent.invoke("Analyze this data...")
agent.invoke("Write a report...")

print(tracker.report())
# {"total_input_tokens": 2847, "total_output_tokens": 891, "estimated_cost_usd": 0.0219}

docs.title: PrintingCallbackHandler
docs.text:
Built-in handler that prints all events:

from strands_agents_jsii import PrintingCallbackHandler

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="Be helpful.",
    callback_handler=PrintingCallbackHandler(),
))

docs.title: Conversation Management
docs.text: Control how conversation history is managed across agent interactions.

docs.title: Managers
docs.text: Manager Behavior Use Case SlidingWindowConversationManager Keeps last N messages Long conversations NullConversationManager Keeps all messages (no trimming) Short tasks

docs.title: Sliding Window
docs.text:
Keeps the most recent messages within a token/message limit:

from strands_agents_jsii import (
    StrandsAgent, AgentConfig, SlidingWindowConversationManager,
    BedrockModelProvider, BedrockModelConfig,
)

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
    system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    conversation_manager=SlidingWindowConversationManager(
        window_size=20  # Keep last 20 messages
    ),
))

# First 20 messages are retained, older ones are dropped
for i in range(50):
    response = agent.invoke(f"Message {i}: tell me something new")
    # Context window stays manageable

docs.title: Null Manager
docs.text:
No conversation trimming — all messages are kept:

from strands_agents_jsii import NullConversationManager

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="...",
    conversation_manager=NullConversationManager(),
))

Context limits

With NullConversationManager, you're responsible for managing context length. If messages exceed the model's context window, you'll get errors.

docs.title: Multi-Turn Conversations
docs.text:
Both managers support multi-turn conversations:

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="You are a math tutor.",
    conversation_manager=SlidingWindowConversationManager(20),
))

# Turn 1
agent.invoke("What is a derivative?")

# Turn 2 — agent remembers context
agent.invoke("Can you give me an example?")

# Turn 3 — still has context
agent.invoke("Now explain the chain rule")

docs.title: Choosing a Manager
docs.text: Scenario Manager Window Size One-shot queries NullConversationManager N/A Chat assistant SlidingWindowConversationManager 20-50 Long analysis SlidingWindowConversationManager 50-100 Tool-heavy tasks SlidingWindowConversationManager 10-20

docs.title: Multi-Agent Patterns
docs.text: Orchestrate multiple agents for complex workflows.

docs.title: Agent-as-Tool (Recommended)
docs.text:
The simplest multi-agent pattern — wrap agents as tools:

from strands_agents_jsii import StrandsAgent, AgentConfig, AgentTool, BedrockModelProvider, BedrockModelConfig

def make_agent(prompt):
    return StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
        model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
        system_prompt=prompt,
    ))

# Specialists
sql_agent = make_agent("You write SQL queries. Return only the query.")
python_agent = make_agent("You write Python code. Return only the code.")
docs_agent = make_agent("You write documentation. Be thorough.")

# Manager
manager = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
    system_prompt="Route tasks to the right specialist.",
    tools=[
        AgentTool("sql_expert", "Write SQL queries", sql_agent),
        AgentTool("python_expert", "Write Python code", python_agent),
        AgentTool("docs_writer", "Write documentation", docs_agent),
    ],
))

response = manager.invoke("Create a user table in SQL, Python ORM model, and API docs")

docs.title: 1. Router (Manager delegates)
docs.text:
graph TD
    U[User] --> M[Manager]
    M -->|task A| A[Agent A]
    M -->|task B| B[Agent B]
    A --> M
    B --> M
    M --> U

Best for: Tasks with clear domain boundaries

docs.title: 2. Pipeline (Sequential handoff)
docs.text:
graph LR
    U[User] --> A[Research] --> B[Draft] --> C[Edit] --> U
# Each step feeds into the next
research = manager.invoke("Research topic X")
draft = manager.invoke(f"Write article from: {research.text}")
final = manager.invoke(f"Edit this draft: {draft.text}")

Best for: Content creation, data processing pipelines

docs.title: 3. Debate (Multiple perspectives)
docs.text:
# Get opposing views, then synthesize
response = manager.invoke(
    "Get the optimist, pessimist, and realist views on AI regulation, "
    "then write a balanced summary."
)

Best for: Decision making, analysis

docs.title: Nesting Depth
docs.text:
Agents can be nested multiple levels:

CEO Agent
├── CTO Agent (tool)
│   ├── Backend Agent (tool)
│   └── Frontend Agent (tool)
└── CFO Agent (tool)
    └── Analytics Agent (tool)

Set max_cycles

Always set max_cycles on agents to prevent infinite delegation loops. A good default is 5-10 for inner agents, 20-50 for outer agents.

docs.title: Patterns
docs.text: Advanced patterns for building production AI agent systems.

docs.title: Available Patterns
docs.text: Pattern Description Page Multi-Agent Delegate tasks to specialist sub-agents → Conversation Management Control context window and memory → Callback Handlers Hook into agent lifecycle events →

docs.title: Pattern Selection
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[What do you need?] --> B{Complex task?}
    B -->|Single domain| C[Single Agent + Tools]
    B -->|Multiple domains| D[Multi-Agent Delegation]
    A --> E{Long conversations?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Sliding Window Manager]
    E -->|No| G[Null Manager]
    A --> H{Observability?}
    H -->|Logging| I[Callback Handler]
    H -->|Metrics| I
    H -->|Cost tracking| I

docs.title: Anthropic
docs.text: Direct Anthropic API access via REST.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import AnthropicModelProvider, AnthropicModelConfig

model = AnthropicModelProvider(AnthropicModelConfig(
    model_id="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens=4096,
    temperature=0.7,
))

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string claude-sonnet-4-20250514 Anthropic model ID max_tokens number 4096 Max output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature

docs.title: Available Models
docs.text: Model ID Claude Opus 4 claude-opus-4-20250514 Claude Sonnet 4 claude-sonnet-4-20250514 Claude Haiku 3.5 claude-3-5-haiku-20241022

docs.title: Amazon Bedrock
docs.text: The primary provider for production workloads. Uses AWS SDK via subprocess.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# Configure AWS credentials
aws configure
# Or environment variables
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIA...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
export AWS_REGION=us-west-2

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import (
    BedrockModelProvider, BedrockModelConfig,
    StrandsAgent, AgentConfig,
)

# Minimal
model = BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig(
    "us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0"
))

# Full configuration
model = BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig(
    model_id="us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0",
    region="us-west-2",
    max_tokens=4096,
    temperature=0.7,
    top_p=0.9,
))

agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(model=model, system_prompt="Be helpful."))
response = agent.invoke("Hello!")

docs.title: Available Models
docs.text: Model Model ID Claude Opus 4 us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-20250514-v1:0 Claude Sonnet 4 us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0 Claude Haiku 3.5 us.anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0 Llama 3.3 70B us.meta.llama3-3-70b-instruct-v1:0 Mistral Large mistral.mistral-large-2407-v1:0

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string required Bedrock model identifier region string us-east-1 AWS region max_tokens number 4096 Maximum output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature (0-1) top_p number 0.9 Nucleus sampling

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Internally, Bedrock calls use the AWS CLI:

aws bedrock-runtime converse \
  --model-id us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0 \
  --messages '[{"role":"user","content":[{"text":"Hello"}]}]' \
  --region us-west-2

This is executed synchronously via child_process.execSync in the jsii runtime.

docs.title: Cross-Region Inference
docs.text:
Use cross-region model IDs for automatic routing:

model = BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig(
    "us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0",  # "us." prefix = cross-region
    region="us-west-2"
))

docs.title: Google Gemini
docs.text: Google Gemini API via REST.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text: export GOOGLE_API_KEY=AI...

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import GeminiModelProvider, GeminiModelConfig

model = GeminiModelProvider(GeminiModelConfig(
    model_id="gemini-2.5-flash",
    max_tokens=4096,
    temperature=0.7,
))

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string gemini-2.5-flash Gemini model ID max_tokens number 4096 Max output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature

docs.title: Available Models
docs.text: Model ID Gemini 2.5 Flash gemini-2.5-flash Gemini 2.5 Pro gemini-2.5-pro Gemini 2.0 Flash gemini-2.0-flash

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Uses the Gemini generateContent REST API with tool declarations automatically converted to Gemini's function calling format.

docs.title: LiteLLM
docs.text: Universal proxy — use 100+ models with one interface.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# Depends on which provider you route through
export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
# or
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import LiteLLMModelProvider, LiteLLMModelConfig

# OpenAI through LiteLLM
model = LiteLLMModelProvider(LiteLLMModelConfig(
    model_id="openai/gpt-4o"
))

# Anthropic through LiteLLM
model = LiteLLMModelProvider(LiteLLMModelConfig(
    model_id="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
))

# Any LiteLLM-supported model
model = LiteLLMModelProvider(LiteLLMModelConfig(
    model_id="together_ai/meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-chat-hf"
))

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string required LiteLLM model identifier (provider/model format) max_tokens number 4096 Max output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature base_url string Provider default Custom API base URL

docs.title: Why LiteLLM?
docs.text:
• 100+ models through one consistent API
  • Automatic retries and fallbacks
  • Cost tracking across providers
  • Provider/model format makes switching trivial

docs.title: Ollama
docs.text: Run AI agents with local models via Ollama. No API keys needed.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# Install Ollama
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Pull a model
ollama pull qwen3:8b

# Start server
ollama serve

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import OllamaModelProvider, OllamaModelConfig

model = OllamaModelProvider(OllamaModelConfig(
    model_id="qwen3:8b",
    host="http://localhost:11434",
    temperature=0.7,
))

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string qwen3:8b Ollama model name host string http://localhost:11434 Ollama server URL temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature num_predict number 4096 Max output tokens

docs.title: Recommended Models
docs.text:
Model Size Tool Use Best For qwen3:1.7b 1.7B ✅ Quick tasks, low memory qwen3:8b 8B ✅ Good balance qwen3:30b 30B ✅ Complex reasoning llama3.3:70b 70B ✅ Best local quality

Tool calling support

Not all Ollama models support tool calling. Qwen3 and Llama 3.x models work well. Check ollama show <model> for capabilities.

docs.text: Model ID GPT-4o gpt-4o GPT-4o Mini gpt-4o-mini GPT-4.1 gpt-4.1 o3 o3 o4-mini o4-mini

docs.title: Custom Base URL
docs.text:
Works with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint:

model = OpenAIModelProvider(OpenAIModelConfig(
    model_id="my-model",
    base_url="https://my-proxy.com/v1"
))

docs.title: Model Providers
docs.text: The jsii SDK supports 10 model providers, matching the Python Strands Agents SDK.

docs.title: Provider Matrix
docs.text: Provider API Style Auth Streaming Local Amazon Bedrock AWS SDK AWS Credentials ❌ ❌ Anthropic REST API Key ❌ ❌ OpenAI REST API Key ❌ ❌ Google Gemini REST API Key ❌ ❌ Ollama REST None ❌ ✅ Mistral OpenAI-compatible API Key ❌ ❌ LiteLLM OpenAI-compatible Varies ❌ ❌ Writer OpenAI-compatible API Key ❌ ❌ LlamaAPI OpenAI-compatible API Key ❌ ❌ Amazon SageMaker AWS SDK AWS Credentials ❌ ❌

docs.title: Quick Selection Guide
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Choose Provider] --> B{Have AWS?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Need managed?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Amazon Bedrock]
    C -->|Custom model| E[Amazon SageMaker]
    B -->|No| F{Want local?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Ollama]
    F -->|No| H{Preference?}
    H -->|Anthropic models| I[Anthropic Direct]
    H -->|OpenAI models| J[OpenAI]
    H -->|Google models| K[Google Gemini]
    H -->|Any model| L[LiteLLM]

docs.title: Common Pattern
docs.text:
All providers share the same interface:

from strands_agents_jsii import StrandsAgent, AgentConfig

# Any provider works here
agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=provider,  # Swap providers without changing agent code
    system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    tools=[MyTool()],
))

docs.title: Amazon SageMaker
docs.text: Run custom models deployed on SageMaker endpoints.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
# AWS credentials (same as Bedrock)
aws configure
export AWS_REGION=us-west-2

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import SageMakerModelProvider, SageMakerModelConfig

model = SageMakerModelProvider(SageMakerModelConfig(
    endpoint_name="my-llama-endpoint",
    region="us-west-2",
    max_tokens=4096,
))

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description endpoint_name string required SageMaker endpoint name region string us-east-1 AWS region max_tokens number 4096 Max output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature

docs.title: When to Use SageMaker
docs.text:
• Custom fine-tuned models not available on Bedrock
  • Private deployments with VPC isolation
  • Cost optimization for high-throughput workloads
  • GPU instance control (choose specific instance types)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description model_id string palmyra-x5 Writer model ID max_tokens number 4096 Max output tokens temperature number 0.7 Sampling temperature

docs.title: Agent as Tool
docs.text: Wrap an entire agent as a tool for another agent. This enables multi-agent delegation — a manager agent that routes tasks to specialist sub-agents.

docs.title: Using AgentTool
docs.text:
from strands_agents_jsii import (
    StrandsAgent, AgentConfig, AgentTool,
    BedrockModelProvider, BedrockModelConfig,
)

# Create specialist agents
code_agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
    system_prompt="You are an expert Python programmer. Write clean, tested code.",
))

review_agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
    system_prompt="You are a code reviewer. Find bugs and suggest improvements.",
))

# Wrap as tools
code_tool = AgentTool(
    "code_writer",
    "Write Python code for a given task",
    code_agent
)

review_tool = AgentTool(
    "code_reviewer",
    "Review Python code for bugs and improvements",
    review_agent
)

# Manager agent with sub-agents as tools
manager = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=BedrockModelProvider(BedrockModelConfig("us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-20250514-v1:0")),
    system_prompt="""You are a development manager.
    Use code_writer to write code, then code_reviewer to review it.
    Iterate until the code is production-ready.""",
    tools=[code_tool, review_tool],
))

response = manager.invoke("Build a Python function to find prime numbers up to N")
print(response.text)

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    U[User] --> M[Manager Agent]
    M -->|"code_writer(task)"| C[Code Agent]
    M -->|"code_reviewer(code)"| R[Review Agent]
    C -->|result| M
    R -->|feedback| M
    M -->|final answer| U

docs.title: AgentTool API
docs.text:
AgentTool(
    name: str,        # Tool name the model sees
    description: str,  # What this agent-tool does
    agent: StrandsAgent  # The wrapped agent
)

The AgentTool automatically:

  1. Exposes a single query parameter to the parent agent
  2. Forwards the query to the wrapped agent via invoke()
  3. Returns the agent's response text as the tool result

docs.title: Pipeline (Sequential)
docs.text:
researcher = AgentTool("researcher", "Research a topic", research_agent)
writer = AgentTool("writer", "Write content from research", writer_agent)
editor = AgentTool("editor", "Edit and polish content", editor_agent)

pipeline_agent = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="Research, then write, then edit. Use tools in order.",
    tools=[researcher, writer, editor],
))

docs.title: Fan-out (Parallel perspectives)
docs.text:
optimist = AgentTool("optimist", "Analyze positively", optimist_agent)
pessimist = AgentTool("pessimist", "Analyze risks", pessimist_agent)
realist = AgentTool("realist", "Balanced analysis", realist_agent)

analyzer = StrandsAgent(AgentConfig(
    model=model,
    system_prompt="Get all three perspectives, then synthesize.",
    tools=[optimist, pessimist, realist],
))

docs.title: Context-Aware Tools
docs.text: Context-aware tools receive a ToolContext with access to the agent itself. This enables tools that can invoke the agent, read conversation history, or modify agent state.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
import json
from strands_agents_jsii import ContextAwareToolDefinition, ToolSpecification, ToolContext

class SmartTool(ContextAwareToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "smart_tool",
            "A tool that can access agent context",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["query"]
            })
        ))

    def execute_with_context(self, input_json: str, context: ToolContext) -> str:
        params = json.loads(input_json)

        # Access the agent that called this tool
        agent = context.agent

        # Use the agent for sub-tasks
        sub_result = agent.invoke(f"Summarize: {params['query']}")

        return json.dumps({"summary": sub_result.text})

docs.title: ToolContext API
docs.text: Property Type Description agent StrandsAgent The agent instance that invoked this tool

docs.title: Example: Research Tool
docs.text:
A tool that uses the agent itself to analyze data:

class ResearchTool(ContextAwareToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "deep_research",
            "Performs multi-step research using the agent",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "topic": {"type": "string", "description": "Research topic"},
                    "depth": {"type": "number", "description": "Analysis depth 1-3"}
                },
                "required": ["topic"]
            })
        ))

    def execute_with_context(self, input_json, context):
        params = json.loads(input_json)
        topic = params["topic"]
        depth = params.get("depth", 1)

        # Use the agent for analysis
        analysis = context.agent.invoke(
            f"Analyze '{topic}' at depth level {depth}. "
            f"Consider causes, effects, and implications."
        )

        return json.dumps({
            "topic": topic,
            "analysis": analysis.text,
            "tokens_used": analysis.input_tokens + analysis.output_tokens
        })

docs.title: When to Use Context-Aware Tools
docs.text:
Scenario Simple Tool Context-Aware Stateless computation ✅ Overkill API calls ✅ Overkill Sub-agent delegation ❌ ✅ Reading conversation history ❌ ✅ Dynamic tool composition ❌ ✅

Recursive agent calls

Context-aware tools can call context.agent.invoke() which may trigger more tool calls. Set max_cycles on your AgentConfig to prevent infinite loops.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Tools give your agent the ability to take actions in the real world — read files, call APIs, query databases, or run code.

docs.title: How Tools Work
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Agent
    participant Model
    participant Tool

    User->>Agent: "What's the weather?"
    Agent->>Model: messages + tool definitions
    Model-->>Agent: tool_use: get_weather({city: "Tokyo"})
    Agent->>Tool: execute('{"city":"Tokyo"}')
    Tool-->>Agent: '{"temp":"22°C"}'
    Agent->>Model: messages + tool_result
    Model-->>Agent: "It's 22°C in Tokyo"
    Agent-->>User: "It's 22°C in Tokyo"

docs.title: Tool Types
docs.text: Type Class Use Case Simple Tool ToolDefinition Standalone function Context-Aware Tool ContextAwareToolDefinition Access to agent state Agent as Tool AgentTool Delegate to sub-agent

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import json
from strands_agents_jsii import ToolDefinition, ToolSpecification

class FileReader(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "read_file",
            "Read contents of a file",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "path": {"type": "string", "description": "File path"}
                },
                "required": ["path"]
            })
        ))

    def execute(self, input_json):
        path = json.loads(input_json)["path"]
        with open(path) as f:
            return json.dumps({"content": f.read()})

docs.title: Tool Schema
docs.text:
Tools define their input schema using JSON Schema:

{
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
        "query": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Search query"
        },
        "limit": {
            "type": "number",
            "description": "Max results (default: 10)"
        }
    },
    "required": ["query"]
}

The model uses this schema to decide when and how to call your tool.

docs.title: Simple Tools
docs.text: The most common tool type. Subclass ToolDefinition and implement execute().

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
import json
from strands_agents_jsii import ToolDefinition, ToolSpecification

class MyTool(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            name="tool_name",
            description="What this tool does",
            input_schema=json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "param1": {"type": "string", "description": "..."},
                    "param2": {"type": "number", "description": "..."}
                },
                "required": ["param1"]
            })
        ))

    def execute(self, input_json: str) -> str:
        params = json.loads(input_json)
        # Your logic here
        result = do_something(params["param1"])
        return json.dumps({"result": result})

docs.title: Calculator
docs.text:
class Calculator(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "calculator", "Evaluate math expressions",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "expression": {"type": "string", "description": "Math expression"}
                },
                "required": ["expression"]
            })
        ))

    def execute(self, input_json):
        import math
        expr = json.loads(input_json)["expression"]
        result = eval(expr, {"__builtins__": {}, "math": math, "sqrt": math.sqrt})
        return json.dumps({"result": result})

docs.title: HTTP Client
docs.text:
class HttpGet(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "http_get", "Fetch a URL",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "url": {"type": "string", "description": "URL to fetch"}
                },
                "required": ["url"]
            })
        ))

    def execute(self, input_json):
        import urllib.request
        url = json.loads(input_json)["url"]
        with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as resp:
            return json.dumps({"status": resp.status, "body": resp.read().decode()[:5000]})

docs.title: Database Query
docs.text:
class SQLQuery(ToolDefinition):
    def __init__(self, db_path):
        self.db_path = db_path
        super().__init__(ToolSpecification(
            "sql_query", "Execute SQL query",
            json.dumps({
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "query": {"type": "string", "description": "SQL SELECT query"}
                },
                "required": ["query"]
            })
        ))

    def execute(self, input_json):
        import sqlite3
        query = json.loads(input_json)["query"]
        conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        cursor = conn.execute(query)
        rows = cursor.fetchall()
        columns = [d[0] for d in cursor.description]
        conn.close()
        return json.dumps({"columns": columns, "rows": rows})

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Tool Design Tips

  1. Clear descriptions — The model uses these to decide when to call your tool
  2. Specific schemas — Include description for each parameter
  3. JSON in, JSON out — Always return valid JSON strings
  4. Error handling — Return error info in JSON, don't throw
  5. Keep tools focused — One tool per capability
